subject: criminal_law
topic_heading: Pre-Trial Procedure — Guilty Pleas and Election
subtopic_heading: Guilty Plea Requirements — s. 606 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: Marcus is charged with fraud over $5,000. At his first appearance in the Ontario Court of Justice, he tells the judge he wants to plead guilty. Defence counsel advises that the court must conduct an inquiry before accepting the plea.
call_of_question: Under s. 606(1.1) of the Criminal Code, which of the following must the court be satisfied of before accepting a guilty plea?

options.A: That the accused has obtained independent legal advice and that the Crown consents to the plea.
options.B: That the accused is making the plea voluntarily, understands the nature of the charge, understands the consequences of the plea, and that the facts admitted are sufficient to support a conviction.
options.C: That the accused's counsel has reviewed the disclosure and that the accused has no outstanding Charter applications.
options.D: That the accused understands the nature of the charge and that the Crown has provided a sentencing recommendation.

explanation: Section 606(1.1) of the Criminal Code requires the court to be satisfied of four things before accepting a guilty plea: (a) the accused is making the plea voluntarily; (b) the accused understands the nature of the charge; (c) the accused understands that the plea is an admission of the essential elements of the offence; (d) the accused understands the nature and consequences of the plea, including any direct consequences. The court must also be satisfied that the facts admitted by the accused are sufficient to support a conviction for the offence to which the accused is pleading guilty. Crown consent and independent legal advice are not statutory prerequisites under s. 606(1.1).
why_A_wrong: Section 606(1.1) does not require proof of independent legal advice or Crown consent as conditions for accepting a guilty plea. The section focuses on the voluntariness and comprehension of the accused, not procedural consent mechanisms.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 606(1.1) requires the court to confirm voluntariness, understanding of the charge, understanding of the consequences, and factual sufficiency before accepting a guilty plea.
why_C_wrong: Disclosure review and the absence of outstanding Charter applications are not preconditions under s. 606(1.1). The inquiry centres on the accused's understanding and voluntariness, not on the state of the proceedings.
why_D_wrong: While the court inquires about the accused's understanding of the charge, a Crown sentencing recommendation is not a precondition under s. 606(1.1). The four requirements focus on the accused's subjective comprehension and voluntariness.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Guilty Plea Requirements — s. 606
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 606(1) and s. 606(1.1)

tested_concepts: understanding nature and consequences of plea

subject: criminal_law
topic_heading: Pre-Trial Procedure — Guilty Pleas and Election
subtopic_heading: Guilty Plea Requirements — s. 606 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: Priya is charged with assault causing bodily harm. She wishes to plead guilty to the lesser offence of common assault. The Crown opposes the proposed plea to the lesser offence.
call_of_question: Under the Criminal Code, may the court accept Priya's guilty plea to the lesser offence of common assault over the Crown's objection?

options.A: Yes — the court has an inherent jurisdiction to accept any plea the accused offers, regardless of Crown consent.
options.B: Yes — s. 606(4) permits the court to accept a plea to a lesser included offence at any time, without requiring Crown consent.
options.C: No — under s. 606(4), acceptance of a guilty plea to a lesser or included offence requires the consent of the Crown.
options.D: No — the court must enter a not guilty plea on behalf of the accused and proceed to trial if the Crown refuses to consent.

explanation: Section 606(4) of the Criminal Code provides that if an accused pleads not guilty to the offence charged but guilty to some other offence arising out of the same transaction, whether or not it is an included offence, the court may, with the consent of the Crown, accept such a plea. Crown consent is a mandatory prerequisite under s. 606(4). The court cannot impose acceptance of the lesser plea over the Crown's objection. If Crown consent is withheld, the court enters a plea of not guilty to the charge as laid.
why_A_wrong: Inherent jurisdiction does not override the express statutory requirement in s. 606(4) that Crown consent is required for acceptance of a plea to a lesser or included offence. The statute governs.
why_B_wrong: Section 606(4) does require Crown consent. The court does not have unilateral authority to accept a plea to a lesser included offence over the Crown's objection.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 606(4) requires Crown consent before the court may accept a guilty plea to a lesser or included offence. The Crown's refusal here blocks acceptance of the common assault plea.
why_D_wrong: The court does not automatically enter a not guilty plea when the Crown withholds consent to a lesser offence plea. The accused may simply plead not guilty to the charge as laid, or the matter proceeds on the original charge. The court does not manufacture a not guilty plea; the accused makes that election.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Guilty Plea to Lesser Offence — s. 606(4)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 606(4)

tested_concepts: s. 606(4) plea to lesser included offence
limits of court's acceptance of guilty pleas

subject: criminal_law
topic_heading: Pre-Trial Procedure — Guilty Pleas and Election
subtopic_heading: Mode of Trial Election — s. 536 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: David is charged with robbery (an offence not listed in s. 553 or s. 469 of the Criminal Code). At his first appearance in the Ontario Court of Justice, the judge asks David how he elects to be tried.
call_of_question: Under s. 536(2) of the Criminal Code, which of the following correctly describes the three modes of trial available to David?

options.A: Provincial court judge alone; Superior Court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury.
options.B: Provincial court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury; Court of Appeal.
options.C: Ontario Court of Justice jury; Superior Court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury.
options.D: Federal court judge alone; Superior Court judge alone; Superior Court judge and jury.

explanation: Section 536(2) of the Criminal Code provides that an accused charged with an indictable offence (other than one listed in s. 553 or s. 469) may elect to be tried by: (1) a provincial court judge (Ontario Court of Justice) sitting alone; (2) a judge of a superior court of criminal jurisdiction sitting alone (without a jury); or (3) a court composed of a judge of a superior court and a jury. These are the only three modes available under s. 536. The Court of Appeal, federal courts, and provincial court juries are not modes of trial election under s. 536.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 536(2) provides three modes: provincial court judge alone, superior court judge alone, and superior court judge and jury.
why_B_wrong: The Court of Appeal is an appellate tribunal, not a mode of first-instance trial election. Section 536(2) offers three trial modes, none of which is the Court of Appeal.
why_C_wrong: Provincial courts in Ontario do not have jury trial jurisdiction. Jury trials occur only in the Superior Court. The three modes under s. 536(2) do not include a provincial court jury option.
why_D_wrong: Federal courts are not part of the criminal trial election framework under s. 536. The provincial court (Ontario Court of Justice) and the Superior Court of Justice are the relevant trial courts in Ontario.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Mode of Trial Election — s. 536
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2)

subject: criminal_law
topic_heading: Pre-Trial Procedure — Guilty Pleas and Election
subtopic_heading: Mode of Trial Election — s. 536 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: Fatima is charged with an offence listed in s. 536(2) of the Criminal Code. She initially elects trial by provincial court judge. Four months later, before the preliminary inquiry stage has concluded, she wishes to change her election to trial by superior court judge alone.
call_of_question: Under the Criminal Code, may Fatima change her election, and if so, what is required?

options.A: No — an election is irrevocable once made; Fatima must proceed before the provincial court judge.
options.B: Yes — Fatima may re-elect as of right at any time before the trial commences, without conditions.
options.C: Yes — under s. 561, Fatima may re-elect to a superior court mode before the end of the preliminary inquiry with the consent of the Crown, or after the preliminary inquiry is complete.
options.D: Yes — Fatima may re-elect at any time after indictment, but only with leave of the court.

explanation: Section 561 of the Criminal Code governs re-election of mode of trial. An accused who has elected trial by provincial court judge may re-elect to a superior court mode (judge alone or judge and jury) at various stages. Where a preliminary inquiry has not been waived, s. 561 permits re-election before the end of the preliminary inquiry with the consent of the Crown, or without Crown consent after the preliminary inquiry is completed and before the trial begins. The precise procedural steps and timing are governed by s. 561 and related provisions. An election is not irrevocable; however, re-election requires compliance with the statutory process in s. 561, not an unrestricted as-of-right change at any time.
why_A_wrong: Elections under s. 536 are not irrevocable. Section 561 provides a statutory mechanism for re-election, subject to timing and consent requirements.
why_B_wrong: Re-election is not available as of right at any time. Section 561 imposes conditions — in particular, Crown consent may be required depending on the stage of proceedings — and the process must comply with the prescribed procedure.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 561 governs re-election and permits it before the end of the preliminary inquiry with Crown consent, or after the preliminary inquiry without Crown consent, subject to timing rules.
why_D_wrong: Re-election under s. 561 does not require leave of the court. The mechanism is statutory and depends on Crown consent at certain stages, not judicial leave. Linking re-election to post-indictment timing as the exclusive window is also incorrect.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Re-Election of Mode of Trial — s. 561
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2), s. 561

tested_concepts: Crown consent for re-election before PI ends

subject: criminal_law
topic_heading: Pre-Trial Procedure — Guilty Pleas and Election
subtopic_heading: Preliminary Inquiry — Purpose and Committal Standard
fact_pattern: At the conclusion of a preliminary inquiry for a murder charge, defence counsel argues that the Crown's evidence is insufficient to commit the accused to trial. The presiding judge reviews the evidence and concludes that a properly instructed jury, acting reasonably, could return a verdict of guilty.
call_of_question: Based on this conclusion, what order must the preliminary inquiry judge make?

options.A: Discharge the accused, because the evidence does not prove guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.
options.B: Commit the accused to stand trial, because the evidence meets the standard of whether a properly instructed jury could return a verdict of guilty.
options.C: Adjourn the hearing and direct the Crown to produce additional evidence before a committal order may be made.
options.D: Reserve judgment and refer the matter to the Superior Court for a determination of sufficiency.

explanation: The committal standard at a preliminary inquiry is not proof beyond a reasonable doubt. Under s. 548(1) of the Criminal Code, the justice shall order the accused to stand trial if there is sufficient evidence — meaning evidence that, if believed, a jury properly instructed could return a verdict of guilty. The standard is whether the evidence is capable of supporting a conviction, not whether it will or should do so. Because the judge concluded that a properly instructed jury acting reasonably could convict, the committal standard is met and the accused must be committed to stand trial.
why_A_wrong: Discharge is appropriate only if the evidence is insufficient to commit — i.e., no reasonable jury could convict on the evidence presented. The beyond-a-reasonable-doubt standard applies at trial, not at the preliminary inquiry stage.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The committal standard under s. 548(1) is whether the evidence, if believed, could support a verdict of guilty. The judge's conclusion meets this standard, mandating committal.
why_C_wrong: The preliminary inquiry judge has no authority to direct the Crown to produce additional evidence as a precondition to committal. The inquiry is conducted on the evidence adduced; if the committal standard is met, the accused is committed.
why_D_wrong: The preliminary inquiry judge makes the committal determination independently. There is no mechanism to refer the sufficiency question to the Superior Court. The PI judge's role is to apply the s. 548(1) standard to the evidence before them.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Preliminary Inquiry — Committal Standard
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 548(1); United States of America v. Shephard, [1977] 2 SCR 1067

tested_concepts: distinction between PI standard and trial standard

subject: criminal_law
topic_heading: Pre-Trial Procedure — Guilty Pleas and Election
fact_pattern: Bridget initially elected to be tried by a superior court judge and jury for an indictable fraud charge. A preliminary inquiry has been scheduled but has not yet commenced. Two weeks before the preliminary inquiry, Bridget's lawyer advises her to change to trial by provincial court judge alone because the evidence is weak and a quicker resolution would be beneficial. Crown counsel does not consent to the re-election.
call_of_question: Under s. 561 of the Criminal Code, may Bridget re-elect to provincial court judge alone before the preliminary inquiry begins, and does Crown consent determine the outcome?

options.A: No — re-election is only available after a preliminary inquiry has been held; it cannot occur before the PI begins.
options.B: Yes — Bridget may re-elect without Crown consent because re-elections before the preliminary inquiry are freely available to the accused.
options.C: Yes — but only with Crown consent, which Bridget does not have; without Crown consent, the re-election cannot proceed before the preliminary inquiry.
options.D: No — the re-election must occur at the first appearance; it cannot occur two weeks before the PI.

explanation: Section 561 of the Criminal Code governs re-elections. Under s. 561(1)(b), where an accused has elected a mode of trial that requires a preliminary inquiry (such as superior court modes) and wishes to re-elect before the completion of the preliminary inquiry, the accused may do so — but only with the consent of the Crown. If the Crown withholds consent, the accused cannot re-elect before the preliminary inquiry concludes. After the preliminary inquiry is completed (whether by committal or discharge), re-election to a lower mode does not require Crown consent. On these facts, without Crown consent, Bridget cannot re-elect before the PI; she must wait until after the preliminary inquiry concludes.
why_A_wrong: Re-election is available both before and after the preliminary inquiry under s. 561. Section 561 provides mechanisms for re-election at various stages. The restriction is not on timing but on whether Crown consent is required.
why_B_wrong: Re-election before the preliminary inquiry is completed is not freely available without Crown consent. Section 561(1)(b) specifically requires Crown consent for re-elections made before the preliminary inquiry concludes.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 561(1)(b), re-election before the conclusion of the preliminary inquiry requires Crown consent. Without that consent, Bridget must wait until after the PI concludes.
why_D_wrong: There is no rule restricting re-elections to the first appearance. Section 561 provides for re-elections at various stages with the applicable requirements depending on the timing. The issue on these facts is Crown consent, not the timing of the application.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Re-Election Before Preliminary Inquiry — s. 561
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 561(1)(b)

tested_concepts: s. 561(1)(b) re-election before PI requires Crown consent

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Striking Out a Guilty Plea — Basis for Setting Aside
fact_pattern: James pleaded guilty to theft under $5,000 three months ago. He has since retained new counsel who informs him that his prior lawyer failed to advise him that the guilty plea would make him inadmissible to the United States — a consequence that was highly relevant given James's work requires frequent US travel. James now seeks to have his guilty plea struck.
call_of_question: On what basis may an accused apply to strike or set aside a guilty plea after it has been entered and accepted?

options.A: An accused may strike a guilty plea only if the Crown agrees to the withdrawal; otherwise the plea stands permanently.
options.B: A guilty plea may be struck if the accused can demonstrate that the plea was not voluntary, was not informed (the accused did not understand the nature or consequences of the plea), or that there was a miscarriage of justice.
options.C: An accused may withdraw a guilty plea as of right at any time before sentencing without any reason.
options.D: A guilty plea can only be set aside if the accused was not represented by counsel at the time of the plea.

explanation: Canadian courts have established that a guilty plea may be set aside on appeal or by other application where the accused can demonstrate that the plea was not valid at the time it was entered. The grounds include: (a) the plea was not voluntary (e.g., entered under threats or undue pressure); (b) the plea was not informed — the accused did not understand the nature of the charge, the consequences of the plea, or lacked advice on a material consequence such as immigration consequences; or (c) the circumstances otherwise amount to a miscarriage of justice. The failure by counsel to advise of significant collateral consequences (such as US immigration inadmissibility for a person who travels frequently for work) may constitute an uninformed plea. However, not all collateral consequences need be disclosed; the analysis turns on materiality.
why_A_wrong: Crown consent is not required to set aside a guilty plea. The accused may apply to the court on established legal grounds (involuntariness, lack of information, miscarriage of justice). The Crown's position is relevant but not determinative.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A guilty plea may be set aside if it was involuntary, uninformed (including failure to advise of material consequences), or amounts to a miscarriage of justice.
why_C_wrong: An accused does not have an as-of-right withdrawal of a guilty plea at any time before sentencing. Withdrawal before sentencing is in the court's discretion, and the court must be satisfied there is a valid reason. After sentencing, a formal application with legal grounds is required.
why_D_wrong: Lack of legal representation at the time of the plea is a factor that may bear on voluntariness or lack of understanding, but it is not the exclusive or even primary ground for striking a guilty plea. A represented accused whose counsel failed to advise of material consequences may also have a valid basis to set aside the plea.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Setting Aside a Guilty Plea
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 606; R. v. Wong, 2018 SCC 25 (immigration consequence of plea)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty to advise client of material consequences of guilty plea, including immigration consequences

tested_concepts: R. v. Wong immigration advice duty

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Plea Bargaining — Counsel's Authority to Agree to Facts
fact_pattern: Carson is charged with assault causing bodily harm. His counsel, Ms. Rao, negotiates a plea arrangement with the Crown. The Crown agrees to accept a guilty plea to simple assault and to recommend a suspended sentence, in exchange for an agreed statement of facts that includes the following admission: 'The accused struck the victim twice in the face, causing bruising, and expressed no remorse.' Carson has not reviewed or approved this agreed statement of facts. At the plea hearing, Ms. Rao presents the agreed statement of facts on Carson's behalf without first obtaining Carson's specific instruction on its contents. Carson later complains that he never agreed to the 'no remorse' admission, which he says is false.
call_of_question: Which of the following best describes Ms. Rao's professional responsibility obligation regarding the agreed statement of facts?

options.A: Ms. Rao acted properly — counsel conducting plea negotiations may agree to factual admissions as part of a plea arrangement on behalf of a client without specific instructions, as negotiations are within counsel's general authority to manage the proceeding.
options.B: Ms. Rao acted properly — once the client authorizes counsel to negotiate a plea, counsel has implied authority to agree to any facts necessary to complete the arrangement, including characterizations adverse to the client.
options.C: Ms. Rao breached her professional responsibility obligation — counsel must obtain the client's informed instructions before agreeing to facts in an agreed statement of facts, particularly where those facts are adverse to the client.
options.D: Ms. Rao acted properly — agreed statements of facts in plea proceedings are documents controlled by Crown; defence counsel merely reviews and accepts them, and no separate client instruction is required for each factual admission.

explanation: A client's decision to plead guilty is a fundamental decision that belongs to the client — not counsel. The agreed statement of facts presented at the plea hearing forms part of the record on which the guilty plea is entered and the sentencing proceeds. Admissions in the agreed statement of facts are binding on the accused, may affect the sentence, and may have consequences in related proceedings. Counsel must obtain the client's informed and specific instructions before agreeing to facts on the client's behalf, especially where those facts are adverse to the client (such as the 'no remorse' admission). Ms. Rao's failure to review and obtain approval of the agreed statement from Carson before presenting it at the plea hearing is a breach of her professional responsibility obligations. The client must understand and authorize each material admission.
why_A_wrong: Ethics Override Trap. Counsel's general authority to manage proceedings does not extend to agreeing to factual admissions adverse to the client without specific instruction. The plea itself and the facts supporting it require the client's informed consent. General authority to 'manage the proceeding' does not override the requirement for specific client instruction on substantive admissions.
why_B_wrong: Ethics Override Trap. Authorization to negotiate a plea arrangement does not carry implied authority to agree to any facts counsel deems necessary. The scope of the client's instructions is defined by what the client has actually authorized. A blanket 'negotiate a plea' instruction does not authorize counsel to make specific factual admissions — particularly adverse ones — without the client's review and approval.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Counsel must obtain the client's informed instructions before agreeing to facts in an agreed statement of facts, especially where those facts are adverse to the client. Presenting an agreed statement of facts the client has not reviewed or approved is a breach of professional responsibility.
why_D_wrong: Pragmatic Bluff. The agreed statement of facts is not solely a Crown document that defence 'accepts' passively. Defence counsel is an active participant in drafting and agreeing to its contents. Presenting it at the plea hearing is counsel's act, which requires client instruction. The characterization of counsel's role as mere review and acceptance misstates the nature of counsel's obligations in plea proceedings.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Plea Bargaining — Counsel's Role and Client Instructions
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: LSO Rules of Professional Conduct, r. 5.1-1 (competence), r. 3.2-9 (client decision on plea); R. v. Anthony-Cook, 2016 SCC 43

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: counsel acting without client instruction on plea facts

tested_concepts: client's authority over plea decisions and plea facts
counsel's obligation to obtain specific instructions before agreeing to adverse facts
scope of counsel's authority in plea negotiations
professional responsibility in criminal plea proceedings

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: Wesley is charged with fraud over $5,000. He elects to be tried by a superior court judge and jury. His lawyer tells him he is entitled to a preliminary inquiry if he wants one.
call_of_question: Under the Criminal Code (as amended in 2019), when is a preliminary inquiry available to an accused who has elected or is deemed to have elected trial in superior court?

options.A: A preliminary inquiry is available as of right to any accused who elects to be tried in superior court.
options.B: A preliminary inquiry is available only if the maximum sentence for the offence is 14 years or more, or the accused is charged with an offence listed in s. 469.
options.C: A preliminary inquiry is available as of right to all accused persons regardless of the offence charged.
options.D: Preliminary inquiries were abolished by the 2019 Criminal Code amendments; no preliminary inquiry is available for any offence.

explanation: The 2019 Criminal Code amendments (Bill C-75) significantly restricted access to preliminary inquiries. Under s. 535 as amended, a preliminary inquiry is available only for accused persons charged with an offence for which the maximum punishment is imprisonment for a term of 14 years or more, or for an offence listed in s. 469. Fraud over $5,000 carries a maximum of 14 years under s. 380(1)(a), so Wesley would be entitled to request a preliminary inquiry. However, an accused charged with offences carrying lesser maximums (e.g., fraud under $5,000, assault causing bodily harm, etc.) can no longer request a preliminary inquiry following the 2019 amendments.
why_A_wrong: Since 2019, a preliminary inquiry is not available as of right to all accused who elect superior court. The right is now restricted to offences with a maximum penalty of 14 years or more, or s. 469 offences.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 535 as amended in 2019, preliminary inquiries are restricted to offences with a maximum of 14 years or more, or s. 469 listed offences.
why_C_wrong: The 2019 amendments eliminated the as-of-right preliminary inquiry for all offences. Only the specified high-penalty and s. 469 offences retain preliminary inquiry eligibility.
why_D_wrong: Preliminary inquiries were not abolished by the 2019 amendments — they were restricted. Preliminary inquiries remain available for offences with maximum penalties of 14 years or more and s. 469 offences.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Preliminary Inquiry — Eligibility After 2019 Amendment
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 535 (as amended by S.C. 2019, c. 25)

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Preliminary Inquiry — Discharge at Committal Stage
fact_pattern: At a preliminary inquiry for aggravated assault, the Crown's sole witness recants her identification of the accused and states she cannot now say who attacked her. No other identification evidence is adduced. Defence moves for a discharge. The preliminary inquiry judge finds there is no evidence capable of supporting a conviction on identification.
call_of_question: What order should the preliminary inquiry judge make, and what is the effect of that order?

options.A: Commit the accused to stand trial because the recantation goes to credibility, not to the legal sufficiency of the evidence.
options.B: Discharge the accused; the discharge is equivalent to an acquittal and the Crown cannot re-lay the charge.
options.C: Discharge the accused under s. 548(1)(b); a discharge at the preliminary inquiry stage is not an acquittal and the Crown may re-lay the charge by direct indictment.
options.D: Refer the matter to the superior court for a determination of whether the evidence is sufficient.

explanation: Under s. 548(1) of the Criminal Code, where the evidence adduced is not sufficient to put the accused on trial, the justice shall order the accused discharged. On the facts, with the sole identification witness having recanted and no other identification evidence, there is no evidence on which a properly instructed jury could convict — the committal standard is not met and discharge is mandatory. However, a discharge at the preliminary inquiry is not an acquittal for the purpose of double jeopardy. The Crown retains the ability to lay a fresh charge or to proceed by way of direct indictment (s. 577) to bring the accused to trial without a further preliminary inquiry, if additional evidence emerges or the Crown decides to exercise that discretion.
why_A_wrong: The recantation is not merely a credibility issue — it is the complete absence of identification evidence. The committal standard requires that there be some evidence capable of supporting a conviction. Where there is no identification evidence, the legal threshold is not met regardless of credibility arguments.
why_B_wrong: A discharge at a preliminary inquiry is not equivalent to an acquittal. Double jeopardy under s. 11(h) of the Charter protects against a second trial for the same offence after acquittal or conviction. A preliminary inquiry discharge is not a final verdict and does not engage double jeopardy protection.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The accused must be discharged under s. 548(1)(b) where the evidence is insufficient. The discharge is not an acquittal; the Crown may re-charge or proceed by direct indictment.
why_D_wrong: The preliminary inquiry judge makes the committal/discharge determination independently under s. 548. There is no mechanism to refer the sufficiency question to the superior court; that is the PI judge's function.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Preliminary Inquiry — Discharge and Its Effect
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 548(1)(b), s. 577; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(h)

tested_concepts: Crown may re-charge after PI discharge

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Hybrid Offences — Effect of Crown's Summary Election on Accused's Right to Elect
fact_pattern: Carla is charged with mischief under $5,000 — a hybrid offence. The Crown elects to proceed summarily. Carla's lawyer advises her that the Crown's election changes what mode of trial options are available to Carla.
call_of_question: When the Crown elects to proceed summarily on a hybrid offence, does the accused retain the right to elect their mode of trial?

options.A: Yes — the accused always retains the right to elect the mode of trial regardless of the Crown's election.
options.B: No — where the Crown elects to proceed summarily, the matter is tried in the provincial court as a summary conviction proceeding; the accused has no right to elect their mode of trial.
options.C: Yes — the accused may elect to be tried by a superior court judge even where the Crown has elected summarily.
options.D: No — but the accused may appeal the Crown's election to the superior court for review.

explanation: For hybrid offences, the Crown's election to proceed summarily converts the matter into a summary conviction proceeding for all purposes. Summary conviction proceedings are conducted before a provincial court judge without jury, and the accused has no right to elect a mode of trial. The accused's right to elect under s. 536 of the Criminal Code applies only to indictable offences (that are not within the absolute or exclusive jurisdiction of either the provincial or superior court). When the Crown proceeds summarily, the accused is not entitled to elect a superior court or jury trial.
why_A_wrong: The accused's right to elect under s. 536 applies to indictable proceedings, not summary conviction proceedings. Once the Crown elects summarily, the matter is treated as summary conviction and no election right arises for the accused.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. When the Crown elects summarily on a hybrid offence, the matter proceeds as a summary conviction before a provincial court judge; the accused has no s. 536 election right.
why_C_wrong: An accused whose matter is proceeding summarily cannot elect a superior court trial. The superior court election under s. 536 is only available for indictable offences. The Crown's summary election closes that option.
why_D_wrong: There is no appeal mechanism by which an accused can challenge or overturn the Crown's election as to the mode of proceeding for a hybrid offence. The Crown's election is a prosecutorial discretion that courts have consistently declined to review on the merits.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Hybrid Offences — Crown Election Effect
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2); interpretation of hybrid offences

tested_concepts: Crown summary election forecloses accused election
s. 536 election only for indictable offences
accused has no election right on summary proceedings

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: At a preliminary inquiry for robbery, the Crown seeks to rely on a written statement from a witness who is unable to attend. Crown counsel argues the statement is sufficient evidence for the purpose of committal. Defence objects that hearsay evidence cannot support committal.
call_of_question: Under the Criminal Code, may hearsay evidence be admitted and relied upon at a preliminary inquiry to support committal?

options.A: No — the rules of evidence at preliminary inquiries are identical to trial; hearsay evidence is not admissible.
options.B: Yes — s. 540 of the Criminal Code specifically provides that hearsay evidence may be received at a preliminary inquiry if the justice considers it appropriate, even if that evidence would be inadmissible at trial.
options.C: Yes — hearsay evidence is automatically admissible at all preliminary inquiries without restriction.
options.D: No — a preliminary inquiry justice has no authority to admit evidence that would be inadmissible at trial.

explanation: Section 540(7) of the Criminal Code expressly provides that the justice at a preliminary inquiry may receive as evidence any information that would otherwise be admissible as evidence or any other information, if the justice is of the opinion that such other information is reliable. This provision allows the justice to receive evidence — including hearsay — that would not be admissible at trial, provided the justice considers it reliable. The purpose of the preliminary inquiry is to determine whether there is sufficient evidence for committal, not to conduct a full trial on admissibility rules. This is one of the key distinctions between the preliminary inquiry and the trial.
why_A_wrong: The rules of evidence at a preliminary inquiry are not identical to those at trial. Section 540(7) specifically permits the justice to receive evidence that would be inadmissible at trial, making the preliminary inquiry a more flexible proceeding.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 540(7) of the Criminal Code, hearsay and other ordinarily inadmissible evidence may be received at a preliminary inquiry if the justice considers it reliable.
why_C_wrong: The admission of hearsay at a preliminary inquiry is not automatic and without restriction. The justice must be of the opinion that the hearsay information is reliable before admitting it under s. 540(7). Reliability is a precondition, not an automatic grant.
why_D_wrong: Section 540(7) expressly grants the preliminary inquiry justice authority to receive evidence that would be inadmissible at trial. The justice does have this authority, subject to the reliability condition.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Preliminary Inquiry — Admissible Evidence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 540(7)

tested_concepts: distinction between PI and trial evidence rules

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Plea to Included Offence — Basis for Acceptance
fact_pattern: Lia is charged with robbery. She wishes to plead not guilty to robbery but guilty to theft, which she argues is an included offence arising from the same transaction. The Crown consents to this plea arrangement. The trial judge must determine whether to accept the plea to theft.
call_of_question: Assuming the Crown consents under s. 606(4), what additional condition must the court be satisfied of before accepting Lia's guilty plea to theft?

options.A: The court must be satisfied that theft is a lesser included offence of robbery as a matter of law.
options.B: The court need only confirm the Crown's consent; no further inquiry is required once consent is established.
options.C: The court must be satisfied of the same s. 606(1.1) conditions for any guilty plea — that the plea is voluntary, informed, and that the facts admitted are sufficient to support a conviction for theft.
options.D: The court must hold a preliminary inquiry to determine whether the facts support the lesser charge before accepting the plea.

explanation: Under s. 606(4) of the Criminal Code, where the Crown consents, the court may accept a plea of guilty to a lesser or included offence. However, Crown consent is a necessary but not sufficient condition. Before accepting any guilty plea — including a plea to a lesser or included offence — the court must still conduct the s. 606(1.1) inquiry to be satisfied that: (a) the accused is pleading voluntarily; (b) the accused understands the nature of the charge to which they are pleading (here, theft); (c) the accused understands the consequences of the plea; and (d) the facts admitted by the accused are sufficient to support a conviction for the offence (theft). The s. 606(1.1) safeguards apply to all guilty pleas regardless of the mechanism under which they arise.
why_A_wrong: Whether theft is technically an included offence of robbery as a matter of law is relevant to whether s. 606(4) applies, but it is not the additional condition the court must be satisfied of before accepting the plea. The additional condition is the s. 606(1.1) inquiry.
why_B_wrong: Crown consent alone is not sufficient for the court to accept the plea. Section 606(1.1) requires the court to be satisfied of the voluntariness, understanding, and factual basis of the plea regardless of Crown consent. Crown consent enables the process under s. 606(4) but does not supplant the s. 606(1.1) requirements.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Even with Crown consent under s. 606(4), the court must conduct the s. 606(1.1) inquiry — confirming voluntary, informed plea with sufficient factual basis — before accepting the guilty plea to the lesser offence.
why_D_wrong: A preliminary inquiry is not required before accepting a guilty plea to a lesser offence. Preliminary inquiries occur before trial to determine sufficiency of evidence for committal. A guilty plea at the plea stage does not require a preliminary inquiry as a precondition.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Guilty Plea to Lesser Included Offence — s. 606

tested_concepts: s. 606(4) Crown consent — necessary but not sufficient
s. 606(1.1) still applies to lesser offence pleas
combined operation of s. 606(1.1) and s. 606(4)
